Сдейлай лаконичный пересказ на английском языке, рассказывающий об истоии The Teck Turquoise Tiara "The incredible Gloucester tiara collection includes multiple pieces inherited from Queen Mary, including the Teck Turquoise Tiara. Combining turquoises with diamonds in an intricate design, the tiara has been worn by two generations of Gloucester duchesses.


The suite from the Illustrated London News drawings of Queen Mary’s wedding gifts, 1893
 
The piece was originally made for Princess Mary Adelaide of Cambridge, a granddaughter of George III, who married Prince Francis, Duke of Teck. The tiara was a part of a parure of turquoise jewelry that was made for Mary Adelaide around 1850; the set also originally included a necklace, earrings, and three brooches. (Eventually, additional turquoise pieces were added to the suite of jewels.)

 


Queen Mary wears the tiara before its 1912 renovation
 
In 1893, the Duke and Duchess of Teck gave the parure to Princess Mary as a wedding present. Her new husband was, of course, the future King George V, meaning that the turquoises would adorn a British queen consort. Always tinkering with her jewels, the turquoise tiara didn’t escape Mary’s eye for adaptation. The tiara was originally taller, but Mary had its size reduced in 1912 by E. Wolff and Co., who often did work for Garrard.

 

The tiara and is accompanying jewels are displayed with Princess Alice’s wedding gifts
 
Two decades on, she gave the entire set to her new daughter-in-law, Alice, as a wedding present, echoing her own parents’ gift. The suite was included in the elaborate display of Alice’s wedding gifts, which you can learn more about here!

 


Princess Alice, Duchess of Gloucester wears the tiara and jewels at the Dorchester Hotel, 15 November 1955 (Keystone Pictures USA/Alamy)
 
Alice wore the tiara often, and even posed in it for a famous series of photographs taken by Cecil Beaton. Here, she wears the tiara and jewels in 1955 in London for a fashion show at the Dorchester Hotel.

 


Alice wears the tiara, with Prince William of Gloucester and the Queen Mother, during the Dutch state visit, 13 April 1972 (PA Images/Alamy)
 
She continued to wear the tiara throughout her life as a working royal. Above, Alice wears the suite in April 1972 during a state visit from Queen Juliana of the Netherlands. Also pictured here: the Queen Mother (more on her jewels from this occasion here) and Alice’s elder son, Prince William of Gloucester. William died only four months later in a plane crash.

 


JUSTIN TALLIS/AFP/Getty Images
 
Today, the set is worn by Alice’s daughter-in-law, Birgitte, the wife of the current Duke of Gloucester. It’s not her most-worn tiara (that prize probably goes to the Honeysuckle Tiara, another legacy from Queen Mary), but it pops up now and again, as it did in March 2015 for the Guildhall banquet during the Mexican state visit. You’ll recognize quite a few pieces from the parure on Birgitte in the image above, taken during that banquet."

The Teck Turquoise Tiara, part of the esteemed Gloucester tiara collection, features a combination of turquoise and diamonds. Originally made around 1850 for Princess Mary Adelaide of Cambridge, who married Prince Francis, Duke of Teck, this tiara was part of a larger turquoise jewelry set. In 1893, it was given as a wedding gift to Princess Mary, future Queen consort, by her parents. Queen Mary later modified the tiara in 1912, reducing its size for a more contemporary look.

Years later, the tiara and its accompanying jewelry suite were passed down to Mary's daughter-in-law, Alice, Duchess of Gloucester, as a wedding present. Alice frequently wore the tiara, notably at a 1955 fashion show and during a 1972 state visit from Queen Juliana of the Netherlands. Today, the turquoise set is worn by Birgitte, Duchess of Gloucester, making appearances at significant events like the 2015 Guildhall banquet during a Mexican state visit. This ongoing tradition showcases the tiara's lasting legacy and its role in royal history.

Сдейлай лаконичный пересказ на английском языке, рассказывающий об истоии THE IVEAGH TIARA "The tiara takes its name from the couple who gave it to Mary: Lord and Lady Iveagh, Irish aristocrats who would eventually become an earl and a countess. The Iveaghs were members of the famed Guinness family, and the tiara’s name is pronounced like their Irish title: either as “eye-vee,” like the plant, or “eye-vah.” The Iveaghs’ gift was well-used by the new princess. Unusually, Mary wore this tiara throughout her lifetime without making any radical changes to its form — a rarity for a piece owned by a queen who loved to experiment with her jewels. (The tiara looks a bit smaller in old photographs, but that’s because of Queen Mary’s towering hair!)

Embed from Getty Images

When Mary died in 1953, the tiara was inherited by her daughter-in-law, the Duchess of Gloucester. Princess Alice wore it during her tenure as duchess, eventually passing it along to her own daughter-in-law, Birgitte, who is the wife of the current Duke.


ALASTAIR GRANT/AFP/Getty Images
The tiara’s home with the Gloucesters and its delicate floral design has earned it another name; you’ll sometimes see the piece called the “Gloucester Leafage” tiara. Birgitte often pairs the tiara with diamonds or pearls, as she did in October 1999 for a Guildhall banquet in honor of the Chinese president.

Embed from Getty Images

In 2008, the tiara adorned the head of another Gloucester woman: Lady Rose Windsor, the daughter of the current Duke and Duchess. She wore it at her wedding to George Gilman, which took place in the Queen’s Chapel at St. James’s Palace.


CARL COURT/AFP/Getty Images
Today, the tiara only spotted on rare occasions, as Birgitte generally tends to wear other pieces to events like state banquets. But she still does sometimes reach for the tiara, as she did in October 2010 to host the Qatari royals at the Guildhall. She also seems to have chosen the tiara for the 2017 Spanish state dinner.


I was especially delighted to see the Iveagh pop up in public again last week, when Birgitte wore it to the Queen’s Dinner during the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting festivities. The tiara is just an absolute classic. Its balanced, kokoshnik-style design and delicate-yet-solid diamonds make it one of the loveliest in the Gloucester collection."

The Iveagh Tiara, named after its benefactors Lord and Lady Iveagh of the renowned Guinness family, was originally given to Mary, who would become Queen Mary. Unlike many of her other pieces, Mary chose not to alter this tiara significantly over the years, despite her penchant for modifying her jewelry. The tiara features a delicate floral design, leading some to refer to it as the “Gloucester Leafage” tiara.

After Queen Mary's death in 1953, the tiara passed to her daughter-in-law, the Duchess of Gloucester, and has since been worn by the women of the Gloucester family, including Birgitte, the current Duchess, and Lady Rose Windsor, the daughter of the current Duke, who wore it at her wedding in 2008.

Though not frequently seen, the tiara remains a favorite for select occasions. Birgitte has worn it at significant events, including state dinners and a recent appearance at the Queen’s Dinner during the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting. The Iveagh Tiara is celebrated for its classic, kokoshnik-style design and remains a cherished piece in the Gloucester collection.

Сделай лаконичный пересказ на основе следующего текста на английском языке. Оформи его как рассказ о Ladies of England Tiara, убери лишнее, не сокращай интересные факты "It’s officially the month of May—and since the most famous Royal May of all was undoubtedly Queen Mary, we’re going to be devoting several articles to some of her lesser-known jewels this month. Let’s kick things off with a look at a long-lost sparkler: the Ladies of England Tiara.


The Duke of York and Princess May of Teck on their wedding day, 1893
Royal Collection
On July 6, 1893, Princess Victoria Mary of Teck (who was always called “May”) married Prince George, Duke of York, heir to the British throne. (You can read about their very unusual courtship and engagement, as well as May’s wedding jewels here.) The wedding of a future monarch and his consort was celebrated widely, and as you can imagine, incredible wedding gifts were lavished on them both.

 

The Ladies of England Tiara
Folkestone Express and Hythe and Sandgate Advertiser
The incredible wedding gift hall given to the couple was highlighted in newspapers across the country in July 1893. May, in particular, received an astonishing number of fabulous jewels. Among these was a convertible diamond and pearl tiara from Hunt & Roskell, a gift from a committee of 650 women calling themselves the “Ladies of England.” The St. James’s Gazette opined, “One of the most admired gift was that sent by 650 ladies of England. This was a magnificent tiara, capable of being worn also as a necklace, and so adjustable that a part of it may be used as a brooch and the remaining sections as sprays. It was composed of brilliants, with large pearls as drops, with pearl earrings to match.”

The Folkestone Express and Hythe and Sandgate Advertiser wrote, “The exquisite necklet of diamonds and pearls presented by the ‘Ladies of England,’ together with the earrings to match from ‘Devonshire,’ does both their loyalty and their taste infinite credit. The illustration above was also included in the same paper’s July 8, 1893 edition. The matching pearl and diamond earrings, the Ladies of Devonshire Earrings, had been selected as a gift by a different committee (the Ladies of Devonshire, naturally) specifically to coordinate with the Ladies of England Tiara.

 

The Ladies of England Tiara
Grand Ladies Site
Princess May, now Duchess of York, wore the Ladies of England Tiara occasionally in its tiara form. We have several portraits that show her wearing the jewel in this setting, which appear to be different images taken from the same photo session, which dates to the mid-1890s.

 

The Ladies of England Tiara
Wikimedia Commons
In the portraits, she’s wearing the Ladies of England Tiara with the Ladies of Devonshire Earrings. She’s also wearing an incredible pearl choker necklace, featuring eleven rows of small pearls with elaborate diamond spacers, as well as a longer pearl necklace. The Devonshire Earrings now belong to the Queen; the Eleven-Row Pearl Choker Necklace belongs to the Duke and Duchess of Gloucester.

 

The Ladies of England Tiara
Wikimedia Commons
While May often wore the Ladies of England jewel in its tiara form, she also liked to play around with its versatility. Here, at the Devonshire House Ball during the Diamond Jubilee celebrations in July 1897, she wears the jewel, taken off its frame and stretched straight across the neckline of her gown. The event was an enormous costume party. The Duke and Duchess of York were dressed as the 3rd Earl of Cumberland (a courtier of Elizabeth I) and a lady of the court of Marguerite de Valois. (The Princess of Wales, May’s mother-in-law and the most senior royal lady present was naturally in costume as Marguerite.)

You’ll spot a lot of other familiar jewelry on May here. She wore a petite coronet setting of the Girls of Great Britain and Ireland Tiara. The Richmond Brooch is used as a pendant on her pearl necklace. And several more brooches cascade down the front of her bodice: the Kensington Bow Brooch, one of her diamond stomachers, and the Dorset Bow Brooch.

 

The Ladies of England Tiara
Grand Ladies Site
May also wore the jewel frequently as a necklace in the early years. In this portrait, she wears the Ladies of England Necklace with a diamond rivière and her diamond and pearl lattice choker necklace. (The choker is now worn by the Princess Royal.) She also wears the Girls of Great Britain and Ireland Tiara with its original pearl toppers and the Ladies of Devonshire Earrings. Both the Kensington Bow Brooch and the Richmond Brooch are pinned to her bodice.

 

The Ladies of England Tiara
Grand Ladies Site
This painting, showing May wearing the Ladies of England Necklace, was based on another portrait from the same photo session.

 

The Honeysuckle Tiara
Grand Ladies Site
George and May became the Prince and Princess of Wales in 1901, and in 1910, they ascended to the throne as King George V and Queen Mary. Three years later, May decided to have the Ladies of England Tiara dismantled. She used the stones to commission two new tiaras. One was her Honeysuckle Tiara (later reduced in size, and now worn by the Duchess of Gloucester). In this portrait, she wears the honeysuckle tiara with additional diamonds, including the Cullinan III & IV Brooch and the Indian Bangles (all of which now belong to the Queen).

 

The Lover's Knot Tiara
Grand Ladies Site
The other tiara made with the gems from the Ladies of England Tiara was Queen Mary’s Lover’s Knot Tiara, a copy of another jewel (the Cambridge Lover’s Knot Tiara) that belonged to her extended family. She wears the Lover’s Knot here in a 1927 portrait by Arthur Trevethin Nowell. The tiara now belongs to the Queen, and it’s worn today by the Duchess of Cambridge."

The Ladies of England Tiara, a magnificent piece of convertible jewelry capable of being worn as a tiara, a necklace, brooches, and sprays, was gifted to Princess Victoria Mary of Teck, known as "May," by 650 women from England on her wedding to Prince George, Duke of York, in 1893. Accompanied by pearl earrings from the "Ladies of Devonshire," this gift was a testament to the people's loyalty and taste. Princess May, later Duchess of York, was often pictured wearing this tiara and the matching earrings alongside other prominent pieces of jewelry such as the Eleven-Row Pearl Choker Necklace and various brooches.

The tiara's versatility was showcased when May wore it as a necklace across the neckline of her gown at the Devonshire House Ball in 1897. She also frequently donned it as a necklace in the early years of her marriage, pairing it with the Girls of Great Britain and Ireland Tiara and the Ladies of Devonshire Earrings, among other jewels.

However, in 1913, Queen Mary, as she became known, chose to have the Ladies of England Tiara dismantled to create two new tiaras: the Honeysuckle Tiara, now worn by the Duchess of Gloucester, and the Lover's Knot Tiara, familiar today as a favorite of the Duchess of Cambridge. This act of remodelling reflects Queen Mary's love for transforming her jewelry to suit changing styles and occasions, leaving a legacy of exceptional royal tiaras that continue to be admired and worn by the current members of the British Royal Family.

This remarkable Pearl and Diamond Tiara, a creation of Hunt & Roskell, was gifted by a group of 650 'Ladies of England' as a wedding present. It was notable for its versatility, easily convertible from a tiara to a necklace, and could be further disassembled into a corsage ornament, a brooch from its central section, and sprays from its remaining parts. Girls of Great Britain and Ireland Tiara, убери лишнее, не сокращай интересные факты  "Among May’s glittering hoard of wedding presents was the Girls of Great Britain and Ireland Tiara. The tiara takes its name from the committee of young women, led by Lady Eva Greville, who raised money for its creation via subscription. They purchased the tiara from Garrard in June 1893. The tiara, which features festoon and fleur-de-lis designs, was made of diamonds set in silver and gold, and the original 1893 version was topped by fourteen pearls. Inside the custom mahogany box provided by the jeweler, a second frame was also supplied, which allowed the tiara to be worn as a coronet. Even better, it could also be taken off a frame entirely and worn as a necklace.

Incidentally, Lady Eva’s committee was so successful in their appeal to raise funds for the tiara that they ended up with much more money than they needed. The extra funds ended up serving a very good purpose. As the tiara was being made, a maritime disaster shocked Britain: a battleship, the HMS Victoria, accidentally collided with another ship off the coast of Tripoli, killing more than 350 sailors as it sank. May personally requested that the surplus funds from the appeal be sent to a fund that had been established to support the widows and children of the perished sailors.

Although the new Duchess of York didn’t wear the tiara on her wedding day—she wisely chose to wear the diamond fringe presented to her by the groom’s grandmother, Queen Victoria—the tiara quickly became one of her favorite pieces. May wore it on its coronet frame at the famous Devonshire House Ball in 1897. She also wore the tiara when, as Princess of Wales, she attended the coronation of her father-in-law, King Edward VII, in 1902.

Queen Mary was photographed and painted in the tiara and her Garter robes in 1912, shortly after her own coronation. In 1914, however, she decided to tweak the tiara just a bit. She had Garrard remove the pearls from the top of the tiara, replacing them with 13 diamond brilliants. (The pearls were incorporated into her new Lover’s Knot Tiara.) The base of the tiara was also removed around this time so that it could be worn separately as a bandeau. In the 1920s and 1930s, Mary was frequently photographed in the tiara without the signature Garrard diamond-and-dot base.

When Princess Elizabeth, Queen Mary’s granddaughter, married Prince Philip in November 1947, Mary decided to make the tiara one of Elizabeth’s wedding gifts. She presented her with both the tiara and its bandeau base (still separated as two distinct jewels and described as “a hair band” by the Illustrated London News caption), and both went on display at St. James’s Palace in London with the rest of Elizabeth’s bejeweled wedding presents.

Portrait of a young Queen Elizabeth II in gala jewels, 1950s (AFP via Getty Images)
In recognition of the gift, Elizabeth reportedly called the diadem “Granny’s Tiara.” It quickly became a central part of her jewelry wardrobe, first as a princess, and then as Queen Elizabeth II. Above, she wears the tiara in an early portrait.

 Portraits of Elizabeth wearing the tiara in the early months of her reign were used all over the world on stamps, coins, and bank notes, making the jewel particularly closely associated with the young monarch’s image.

 The young Queen brought the tiara with her during her travels across the globe, both before and after the dawn of her reign. Above, she wears the tiara for a state dinner in Quebec during her royal tour of Canada in 1951.

 And here, she wears the tiara during her visit to Sierra Leone in 1961.
 
The tiara also accompanied her on her state visit to Luxembourg in 1976.

And here, she wears the tiara for a dinner on board the royal yacht Britannia with President and Mrs. Reagan during her visit to California in 1983.

In the late 1960s, the bandeau base of the tiara was finally reunited with the rest of the sparkler, bringing the piece back to a more magnificent height. You’ll note the more imposing complete size of the tiara in the photograph above, taken in Singapore in 1989. The Queen has worn the tiara in that more complete form ever since, pairing it with virtually all of the other gala jewels from her collection, including her rubies, the emeralds from the Delhi Durbar Parure, the Brazilian Aquamarines, and even the Kent Amethysts.

 In the twilight years of her reign, the tiara became one of the only diadems that Elizabeth regularly wore. She chose it for several important recent occasions, including the landmark state visit to Ireland in 2011 and the commemoration of the 70th anniversary of D-Day in 2014.
She also packed the tiara for her final foreign state visit, wearing it for a state banquet in Germany in 2015.

 
One of Elizabeth’s final appearances in the jewel took place at the Diplomatic Reception at Buckingham Palace in December 2018.

The tiara’s classic design will surely make it a favorite of royal ladies far into the future, but for years, it was difficult to imagine anyone else wearing the Queen’s signature tiara. The diadem was always “Granny’s Tiara” to Elizabeth, but she undoubtedly made it her own.

 

King Charles III and Queen Camilla of the United Kingdom attend a dinner at Mansion House in London on October 18, 2023 (Aaron Chown/PA Images/Alamy)
King Charles III and Queen Camilla of the United Kingdom attend a dinner at Mansion House in London on October 18, 2023 (Aaron Chown/PA Images/Alamy)
But time marches on. For a special reception at Mansion House in October 2023, Elizabeth’s daughter-in-law, Queen Camilla, made her debut in the tiara. She paired the jewel with another sentimental set of diamonds: the South African Diamonds, which were also gifted to Elizabeth in 1947."

The Girls of Great Britain and Ireland Tiara, renowned for its delicate festoon and fleur-de-lis designs, was a wedding gift to Princess Victoria Mary of Teck, later known as Queen Mary, from a group of young women led by Lady Eva Greville. This exceptional piece, purchasable through a fundraising subscription, was crafted by Garrard in June 1893 and features diamonds set in silver and gold, originally topped with fourteen pearls. Its versatility allowed it to be worn as a tiara, a coronet, or even a necklace, thanks to the inclusion of a second frame.

The fundraising was so prosperous that the surplus was generously donated at May's request to support the families affected by the HMS Victoria maritime disaster. Queen Mary, although not wearing it at her wedding, cherished the tiara throughout her life, making subtle modifications over time, such as replacing the pearl toppers with diamonds in 1914 to create a new look and versatility.

The tiara was gifted to Princess Elizabeth by Queen Mary in 1947, becoming a staple in Elizabeth's royal wardrobe and earning the affectionate nickname "Granny’s Tiara." Over the years, Queen Elizabeth II donned this tiara at numerous significant events across the globe, including key moments in her reign like the state visit to Ireland in 2011 and the D-Day 70th anniversary in 2014. It symbolized her image on stamps, coins, and banknotes worldwide, enhancing its association with the British monarchy.

In the later years of Queen Elizabeth II’s reign, the tiara remained a favored choice for important occasions, its classic design guaranteeing its continued use by future royal generations. Despite being closely tied to Queen Elizabeth, who wore it for one of her last public appearances in 2018, the tiara saw a new era when Queen Camilla wore it at a reception in 2023, pairing it with the sentimental South African Diamonds, also a gift to Elizabeth in 1947, showcasing the tiara's enduring legacy and versatility within the royal family.

do rewrite "The Surrey tiara was a stylized fringe with floral elements and round diamonds making up each “picket” of the piece. To my knowledge, the maker of the piece has never been identified, but like many of Mary’s other wedding tiaras, it was constructed to be convertible. You can see the tiara off its frame and worn as a necklace in the image above; in the photograph, taken in 1905, Mary is wearing it below the Love Trophy Collar (which was made in 1901 by Garrard).

Like the other tiaras we’re looking at this week, the Surrey tiara is no longer a part of the royal collection. In fact, it’s not a part of any collection. Even so, remnants of it linger in Windsor hands today. It was broken up around 1913, and Mary had the stones used in the creation of other jewels. The largest diamonds from this tiara ended up perched atop the Girls of Great Britain and Ireland Tiara (replacing the upright pearls that were transferred to her new Lover’s Knot Tiara). Other diamonds from the dismantled diadem were used in the creation of her Honeysuckle Tiara."

The Surrey tiara featured a unique design with floral motifs and round diamonds arranged in a stylized fringe pattern. While the creator of this tiara remains unidentified, it shared the versatile characteristic common to Queen Mary's wedding tiaras, being convertible. A 1905 photograph shows Queen Mary wearing the tiara as a necklace, beneath the Love Trophy Collar crafted by Garrard in 1901.

Unlike some other royal tiaras, the Surrey tiara no longer exists within or outside the royal collection. Around 1913, it was dismantled, and Queen Mary repurposed its stones for new jewelry pieces. The most significant diamonds from the Surrey tiara were added to the Girls of Great Britain and Ireland Tiara, replacing its original pearl adornments, which were then used in Queen Mary's new Lover’s Knot Tiara. Additionally, some of the diamonds contributed to the making of the Honeysuckle Tiara.

The final, seventh tiara listed among the wedding gifts prompts more questions than it provides answers. It would be more accurate to refer to it not as a tiara, but as a diadem, given that it is described in records as "a handsome diadem of Roman workmanship - from the King and Queen of Italy." There is no reliable information on the appearance of this diadem or whether Queen Mary ever wore it. It is speculated that this might refer to The Laurel-Wreath Tiara, about which little information is available. Regardless, the fate and current location of this diadem remain unknown.

The tiara was photographed on Queen Mary during a 1920s theater visit and was also lent to her niece, Lady Victoria Mary Constance Cambridge, who was photographed wearing it in 1922. Additionally, Queen Mary loaned the tiara to her daughter, Princess Mary, featured in an official portrait session.

While it is speculated that Princess Mary inherited the piece, it was not listed among the jewels sold at her auction. Given that the Harewood family has sold nearly all of their inherited items, if they possessed it, the sale was likely conducted privately. The timeline suggests the tiara may have been only loaned to Princess Mary, evidenced by her 1921 photographs and later images of Queen Mary wearing it in 1924, raising the possibility that it might still reside within the Buckingham Palace vault.

Girls of Great Britain and Ireland Tiara
The Girls of Great Britain and Ireland Tiara, renowned for its delicate festoon and fleur-de-lis designs, was a wedding gift to Princess Victoria Mary of Teck from a group of young women led by Lady Eva Greville. This exceptional piece, purchasable through a fundraising subscription, was crafted by Garrard in June 1893 and features diamonds set in silver and gold, originally topped with fourteen pearls. Its design permitted multiple forms of wear; it could be showcased as a tiara or a coronet when fitted onto its second frame, and as a necklace when removed from the frame entirely.
The fundraising was so prosperous that the surplus was generously donated at May’s request to support the families affected by the HMS Victoria maritime disaster. Queen Mary, although not wearing it at her wedding, cherished the tiara throughout her life, making subtle modifications over time, such as replacing the pearl toppers with diamonds in 1914 to create a new look and versatility.
The tiara was gifted to Princess Elizabeth by Queen Mary in 1947, becoming a staple in Elizabeth’s royal wardrobe and earning the affectionate nickname “Granny’s Tiara.” Over the years, Queen Elizabeth II donned this tiara at numerous significant events across the globe, including key moments in her reign like the state visit to Ireland in 2011 and the D-Day 70th anniversary in 2014. It symbolized her image on stamps, coins, and banknotes worldwide, enhancing its association with the British monarchy.
In the later years of Queen Elizabeth II’s reign, the tiara remained a favored choice for important occasions, its classic design guaranteeing its continued use by future royal generations. Despite being closely tied to Queen Elizabeth, the tiara saw a new era when Queen Camilla wore it at a reception in 2023, pairing it with the sentimental South African Diamonds, also a gift to Elizabeth in 1947.
Diamond Fringe Necklace/Tiara
In 1893, on the occasion of her marriage to Prince George, Duke of York, Queen Victoria presented Princess Mary of Teck with a diamond fringe necklace crafted by Collingwood & Co. Hugh Roberts, in “The Queen’s Diamonds,” notes the piece as a “necklace/tiara … purchased by Queen Victoria in 1893 from Collingwood and Co. as a wedding present for Queen Mary, who wore it on her wedding day.” Though its design closely resembles another of Mary’s wedding presents, the County of Surrey Tiara, each piece maintains its uniqueness.
While wearing the tiara on her wedding day undoubtedly delighted her grandmother-in-law, it seems the piece didn’t become a favored item in her collection. Mary is seen in photographs wearing it as a necklace alongside the Teck turquoise suite, but significant portraits of her donning it as a tiara are absent.
Transformed in 1919, when Mary was Queen Consort, she enlisted Garrard, the crown jewellers, to repurpose the jewels into the now-celebrated Queen Mary’s Fringe Tiara. This creation drew inspiration from the Russian kokoshnik style, renowned in the late 19th and early 20th century.
The Surrey Fringe Tiara
The Surrey tiara featured a unique design with floral motifs and round diamonds arranged in a stylized fringe pattern. While the creator of this tiara remains unidentified, it shared the versatile characteristic common to Queen Mary’s wedding tiaras, being convertible. A 1905 photograph shows Queen Mary wearing the tiara as a necklace, beneath the Love Trophy Collar crafted by Garrard in 1901.
Unlike some other royal tiaras, the Surrey tiara no longer exists within or outside the royal collection. Around 1913, it was dismantled, and Queen Mary repurposed its stones for new jewelry pieces. The most significant diamonds from the Surrey tiara were added to the Girls of Great Britain and Ireland Tiara, replacing its original pearl adornments, which were then used in Queen Mary’s new Lover’s Knot Tiara. Additionally, some of the diamonds contributed to the making of the Honeysuckle Tiara.
The Teck Turquoise Tiara
The turquoise stones were very fashionable during the beginning of the 20th century. Originally made around 1850 for Princess Mary Adelaide of Cambridge, who married Prince Francis, Duke of Teck, this tiara was part of a larger turquoise jewelry set. In 1893, it was given as a wedding gift to Princess Mary, future Queen consort, by her parents. Queen Mary later modified the tiara in 1912, reducing its size for a more contemporary look.
Years later, the tiara and its accompanying jewelry suite were passed down to Mary’s daughter-in-law, Alice, Duchess of Gloucester, as a wedding present. Alice frequently wore the tiara, notably at a 1955 fashion show and during a 1972 state visit from Queen Juliana of the Netherlands. 
By the late 1970s, Princess Alice passed on the Teck Turquoise Tiara and Parure to her daughter-in-law, the current Duchess of Gloucester. While it may not be the tiara she wears most frequently, it makes occasional appearances, such as during the Guildhall banquet in March 2015, which took place amidst the Mexican state visit.
Iveagh Tiara
The Iveagh Tiara, named after its benefactors Lord and Lady Iveagh of the renowned Guinness family, was originally given to Mary, who would become Queen Mary. Unlike many of her other pieces, Mary chose not to alter this tiara significantly over the years, despite her penchant for modifying her jewelry. The tiara features a delicate floral design, leading some to refer to it as the “Gloucester Leafage” tiara.
After Queen Mary’s death in 1953, the tiara passed to her daughter-in-law, the Duchess of Gloucester, and has since been worn by the women of the Gloucester family, including Birgitte, the current Duchess, and Lady Rose Windsor, the daughter of the current Duke, who wore it at her wedding in 2008.
Though not frequently seen, the tiara remains a favorite for select occasions. Birgitte has worn the piece for banquets and official photographs. The Iveagh Tiara is celebrated for its classic, kokoshnik-style design and remains a cherished piece in the Gloucester collection.
Ladies of England Tiara
This remarkable Pearl and Diamond Tiara, a creation of Hunt & Roskell, was gifted by a group of 650 ‘Ladies of England’ as a wedding present. It was notable for its versatility, easily convertible from a tiara to a necklace, and could be further disassembled into a corsage ornament, a brooch from its central section, and sprays from its remaining parts. It was paired with pearl earrings from the “Ladies of Devonshire,” showcasing the donors’ loyalty and refined taste.
After her marriage, the Duchess of York frequently adorned herself with the Ladies of England Tiara, featuring it prominently in portraits alongside her 11-row Pearl Choker in the mid-1890s.
During the same period, Princess Mary also styled the tiara as a necklace for a portrait session that included her wearing the Girls of Great Britain and Ireland Tiara and the City of London Choker, linked to 1896.
Not long after the Accession of King George V and Queen Mary in 1910, the tiara was repurposed. The diamonds from the Ladies of England Tiara were used to craft the Gloucester Honeysuckle Tiara, and its pearls were incorporated into Queen Mary’s Lover’s Knot Tiara.
The Laurel-Wreath Tiara
The final, seventh tiara listed among the wedding gifts prompts more questions than it provides answers. It would be more accurate to refer to it not as a tiara, but as a diadem, given that it is described in records as “a handsome diadem of Roman workmanship - from the King and Queen of Italy.” There is no reliable information on the appearance of this diadem or whether Queen Mary ever wore it. It is speculated that this might refer to The Laurel-Wreath Tiara, about which little information is available. 
The tiara was photographed on Queen Mary during a 1920s theater visit and was also lent to her niece, Lady Victoria Mary Constance Cambridge, who was photographed wearing it in 1922. Additionally, Queen Mary loaned the tiara to her daughter, Princess Mary, featured in an official portrait session.
While it is speculated that Princess Mary inherited the piece, it was not listed among the jewels sold at her auction. Given that the Harewood family has sold nearly all of their inherited items, if they possessed it, the sale was likely conducted privately. The timeline suggests the tiara may have been only loaned to Princess Mary, evidenced by her 1921 photographs and later images of Queen Mary wearing it in 1924, raising the possibility that it might still reside within the Buckingham Palace vault.
Regardless, the fate and current location of this tiara remain unknown.

Despite pleasing her grandmother-in-law on her wedding day, the piece doesn’t appear to have become a favorite in her collection. In 1919, now Queen Consort, Mary had the jewels repurposed by Garrard into the celebrated Queen Mary’s Fringe Tiara, inspired by the Russian kokoshnik style.

The Surrey Fringe Tiara: With a unique design featuring floral motifs and round diamonds in a stylized fringe pattern, the Surrey tiara was another of Queen Mary’s convertible wedding tiaras. No longer existing within any collection, it was dismantled around 1913, with its stones repurposed for new jewelry pieces, including the Girls of Great Britain and Ireland Tiara and Queen Mary’s Lover’s Knot Tiara.

The Teck Turquoise Tiara: Fashionable in the early 20th century, this tiara, originally made for Princess Mary Adelaide of Cambridge, was a wedding gift in 1893 from her parents. Modified in 1912 by Queen Mary for a more contemporary look, it was later passed down to Mary’s daughter-in-law, Alice, Duchess of Gloucester, and remains a part of the royal collection.

Iveagh Tiara: Named after the benefactors Lord and Lady Iveagh of the Guinness family, this tiara features a delicate floral design and is known as the “Gloucester Leafage” tiara. After Queen Mary’s death, it passed to the Duchess of Gloucester and has been worn by family members for select occasions.

Ladies of England Tiara: This versatile Pearl and Diamond Tiara, a creation of Hunt & Roskell, was gifted by a group of 650 ‘Ladies of England.’ It paired with pearl earrings from the “Ladies of Devonshire,” showcasing the donors’ loyalty and refined taste. The diamonds from this tiara were later used to create the Gloucester Honeysuckle Tiara, and its pearls were incorporated into Queen Mary’s Lover’s Knot Tiara.

The Laurel-Wreath Tiara: Described as a “handsome diadem of Roman workmanship” and a gift from the King and Queen of Italy, little is known about this piece, including whether Queen Mary ever wore it. Its fate and current location remain unknown.

как сделать батник, запускающий скрипт на питоне? у меня выдает ошибку "    import requests
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'requests'"

Чтобы создать батник (.bat файл), который запускает скрипт на Python, у вас должно быть установлено окружение Python, и все необходимые библиотеки должны быть установлены в этом окружении. Ошибка "ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'requests'" указывает на то, что модуль `requests` не установлен в вашем Python окружении.

Вот как вы можете устранить проблему и запустить свой скрипт:

1. **Установите библиотеку requests.** Откройте командную строку или терминал и установите библиотеку, выполнив следующую команду:
   ```
   pip install requests
   ```
   Убедитесь, что используете тот же интерпретатор Python, который будет использоваться для запуска вашего скрипта.

2. **Создайте файл .bat.** Откройте блокнот или любой текстовый редактор и напишите команду для запуска вашего скрипта на Python. Например, если ваш скрипт называется `script.py` и находится в `D:\MyScripts`, а Python установлен в `C:\Python39`, то ваш .bat файл должен выглядеть так:
   ```
   @echo off
   C:\Python39\python.exe D:\MyScripts\script.py
   pause
   ```
   Замените пути на те, которые соответствуют вашему расположению Python и скрипта.

3. **Сохраните файл с расширением .bat.** Например, `run_script.bat`. Убедитесь, что при сохранении в блокноте выбран тип файла "Все файлы" и кодировка - "ANSI" или "UTF-8 без BOM", если ваш скрипт содержит символы не из ASCII.

4. **Запустите .bat файл.** Дважды кликните по файлу .bat, чтобы запустить ваш скрипт на выполнение.

Если после этого у вас всё ещё возникает ошибка "ModuleNotFoundError", убедитесь, что вы установили библиотеку `requests` и запускаете скрипт с правильной версией Python, для которой была выполнена установка. Есть вероятность, что в системе установлено несколько версий Python, и `pip` ссылается не на ту, что используется в .bat файле. В этом случае можно указать версию `pip`, соответствующую вашему интерпретатору, например, `pip3.9 install requests` или использовать полный путь к интересующему вас `pip`, например, `C:\Python39\Scripts\pip.exe install requests`.
как узнать, какой интерпретатор Питон запускается в бат-файле?

Чтобы узнать, какой интерпретатор Python запускается при выполнении вашего бат-файла, вы можете использовать команду, которая выводит версию Python и путь к интерпретатору, используемому в бат-файле. Вот шаги, чтобы это сделать:

1. Откройте блокнот или любой текстовый редактор и создайте новый файл.

2. Вставьте следующую команду в файл:
   ```
   @echo off
   python -c "import sys; print(sys.executable); print(sys.version)"
   pause
   ```
   Эта команда запускает Python с параметром `-c`, который позволяет выполнить однострочный скрипт. В данном случае скрипт `import sys; print(sys.executable); print(sys.version)` импортирует модуль `sys` и выводит путь к текущему интерпретатору Python (`sys.executable`) и его версию (`sys.version`).

3. Сохраните файл с расширением `.bat`, например, `check_python.bat`. При сохранении убедитесь, что в блокноте выбран "Все файлы" в качестве типа файла.

4. Запустите созданный бат-файл, дважды кликнув по файлу.

После запуска .bat файла откроется окно командной строки, в котором будет выведен путь к интерпретатору Python и его версия, используемые по умолчанию в системе или в той среде, откуда был запущен бат-файл. Если в вашей системе установлено несколько версий Python и вы хотите убедиться, что бат-файл запускает правильную версию, используйте полный путь до интерпретатора Python в вашем бат-файле (например, `C:\Python39\python.exe`) вместо простого `python`.

Это даст вам точное представление о том, какая версия Python используется при запуске скриптов с помощью бат-файла в вашей системе.
как узнать, какой интерпретатор Питон запускается в командной строке

Чтобы узнать, какой интерпретатор Python используется в командной строке вашей системы, вы можете выполнить несколько простых команд. Эти команды покажут вам путь к исполняемому файлу Python и его версию. Вот как это сделать:

1. **Откройте командную строку.**
   - На Windows нажмите `Win + R`, введите `cmd` и нажмите `Enter`.
   - На macOS откройте `Terminal` через `Finder` > `Applications` > `Utilities` > `Terminal`.
   - На Linux откройте терминал через меню приложений или используя сочетание клавиш, которое может варьироваться в зависимости от дистрибутива.

2. **Введите следующую команду и нажмите `Enter`:**
   ```
   python --version
   ```
   Эта команда покажет версию Python, который используется в качестве основного интерпретатора в вашей системе.

3. **Для получения пути к исполняемому файлу Python, используйте команду:**
   - На Windows или Linux:
     ```
     which python
     ```
     или
     ```
     where python
     ```
     (используйте `where` на Windows и `which` на большинстве дистрибутивов Linux и macOS).

   - На macOS или в некоторых дистрибутивах Linux:
     ```
     which python
     ```
   Эта команда покажет полный путь к исполняемому файлу Python.

Если у вас установлено несколько версий Python, например, Python 2 и Python 3, вы можете попробовать выполнить команду `python2 --version` или `python3 --version` для получения информации о конкретной версии. Аналогично, использование `which python3` или `where python3` (в зависимости от вашей ОС) покажет путь к исполняемому файлу для Python 3.

Эти действия помогут вам узнать, какая версия Python используется по умолчанию в командной строке и где находится соответствующий исполняемый файл.

Я не понимаю, в командной строке и в bat-файле используется одна и та же версия Питона. При запуске скрипта в командной строке все работает отлично, а при запуске того же скрипта в bat-файле, выдается ошибка, что нет модуля (" import requests ModuleNotFoundError: No module named ‘requests’")

Если скрипт Python работает нормально, когда вы запускаете его из командной строки, но вы получаете ошибку "ModuleNotFoundError: No module named ‘requests’" при попытке запуска через bat-файл, это, скорее всего, связано с различиями в окружении или версии Python, используемых в этих двух случаях.

Возможные причины и способы их устранения:

1. **Разные версии Python**. Возможно, в командной строке по умолчанию используется одна версия Python (которая имеет установленный модуль `requests`), а bat-файл запускает скрипт через другую версию.

   - **Решение**: Укажите полный путь к исполняемому файлу Python, который используете в командной строке, в вашем bat-файле. Это можно сделать, выполнив команду `where python` (на Windows) или `which python` (на macOS и Linux) в командной строке для определения пути к интерпретатору Python. Затем, используйте этот путь в вашем bat-файле. Например:
     ```bat
     @echo off
     C:\Users\YourUsername\AppData\Local\Programs\Python\Python39\python.exe yourscript.py
     pause
     ```

2. **Различия в переменных среды**. При запуске из командной строки окружение может включать определенные настройки переменных среды, которые не применяются при запуске через bat-файл.

   - **Решение**: Убедитесь, что путь к нужной версии Python и путь к `Scripts` этой версии Python добавлены в переменную среды `PATH`. Это можно проверить и изменить в настройках системы (на Windows: Панель управления → Система → Дополнительные параметры системы → Переменные среды).

3. **Виртуальное окружение**. Если вы используете виртуальное окружение для запуска скрипта из командной строки, модуль `requests` может быть установлен только в этом виртуальном окружении, и bat-файл может не активировать это окружение автоматически.

   - **Решение**: Активируйте виртуальное окружение прямо в bat-файле перед запуском скрипта. Сделать это можно, добавив команду активации виртуального окружения в начало bat-файла. Например:
     ```bat
     @echo off
     call C:\path\to\your\venv\Scripts\activate.bat
     python yourscript.py
     pause
     ```
Главное в решении этой проблемы — убедиться, что bat-файл использует точно такую же среду исполнения скрипта, как когда вы запускаете его из командной строки.
путь к файлу питона содержит пробелы. Как быть?

Если путь к исполняемому файлу Python содержит пробелы, вам нужно обернуть этот путь в кавычки в вашем bat-файле. Это гарантирует, что путь будет интерпретирован корректно. Вот пример bat-файла, который учитывает пробелы в пути:

```bat
@echo off
"C:\Program Files\Python39\python.exe" "C:\path to your script\yourscript.py"
pause
```

Замените `"C:\Program Files\Python39\python.exe"` на актуальный путь к вашему Python интерпретатору и `"C:\path to your script\yourscript.py"` на путь к вашему скрипту.

Оборачивание пути в кавычки позволяет Windows корректно обрабатывать путь, даже если он содержит пробелы или другие специальные символы.
напиши команду, которая выводит путь к интерпретатору питона в командной строке

Чтобы вывести путь к интерпретатору Python в командной строке, используйте следующую команду Python:

```sh
python -c "import sys; print(sys.executable)"
```

Ввод этой команды в командную строку или терминал и нажатие Enter выведет полный путь к используемому в данный момент исполняемому файлу Python. Это работает в операционных системах Windows, macOS и Linux.

Обратите внимание, что если у вас установлено несколько версий Python или если вы используете виртуальное окружение, эта команда выведет путь к той версии Python или к тому виртуальному окружению, которые активны в вашей текущей сессии командной строки.
как правильно произносить "fleurs de lis". Почему в слове "lis" последняя буква s то произносится, то нет. Допустимы оба варианта?

Фраза "fleurs de lis" происходит из французского языка и переводится как "цветы лилии". В современном французском языке правильное произношение этой фразы звучит примерно как "флёр де ли", где конечный "s" в слове "lis" не произносится. Согласно правилам французской фонетики, конечные согласные часто не произносятся, за исключением случаев, когда следующее слово начинается с гласной (в таких случаях может происходить так называемая "liaison" или связка), что не относится к этому конкретному случаю.

Однако в разных языках и в различных контекстах могут встречаться отклонения от правил изначального произношения. В англоговорящих странах, например, нередко можно услышать произношение "fleurs de lis", где "s" в слове "lis" звучит. Это не соответствует этикету французского произношения, но в контексте английского языка может считаться приемлемым.

Таким образом, если вы говорите на французском языке или стремитесь придерживаться правил французского произношения, рекомендуется говорить "ли" без произношения конечного "s". Если же вы используете эту фразу в англоязычном контексте, вполне возможно, что вы услышите и вариант с произносимым "s", что также может считаться допустимым.
куда ставить ударение в слове repurpose? мне кажется, я слышу, как говорят с ударением как на первый, так и на второй слог.

В английском языке ударение в слове "repurpose" обычно ставят на второй слог: re-PUR-pose. Такое ударение соответствует общему правилу произношения английских слов, где префикс "re-" часто не ударный, особенно в словах, обозначающих действие. 

Впрочем, вариации в произношении могут возникать из-за различий в акцентах, диалектах и личных предпочтениях. Но наиболее распространённым и принятым вариантом будет вариант с ударением на втором слоге.
